title: Goulash
subtitle: Hungarian beef and paprika stew
excerpt: Goulash is the Hungarian national stew: chunks of beef chuck slow-cooked with onion, sweet paprika, caraway seeds, and green pepper until the meat is completely tender and the sauce is thick, dark, and deeply spiced. The paprika is not a garnish — it is the dish. Use a good Hungarian sweet paprika and use more of it than seems right.
sourceNotes:
• George Lang, *The Cuisine of Hungary* (1971). The standard published reference for the paprika proportions and the authentic two-step method of cooking onion before adding beef.
• Claudia Roden, *The Food of Italy* cross-referenced against her broader stew methodology; primary source for the caraway seed and green pepper addition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool fully, transfer to a sealed container, and freeze for up to 3 months. Defrost overnight in the fridge and reheat gently on the hob with a splash of water if the sauce has thickened.
recipe.batchNotes: Goulash scales well. Double the recipe and freeze half.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Goulash improves by a day in the fridge. The paprika flavour deepens overnight. Reheat gently on the hob.

content.text: The thing to understand about goulash is that the paprika is not a seasoning but the structural backbone of the dish. A tablespoon or two gives colour; three tablespoons gives the depth that makes the dish taste like itself. Good Hungarian sweet paprika is mild and fragrant, not hot, and the quantity needed is always more than seems reasonable the first time you make it.

The method is two-part: a very long cook for the onion before any liquid is added, then a slow braise for the beef. The onion must soften to a sweet, nearly jammy state before the paprika goes in — this prevents the paprika from burning and gives the sauce its body. Serve with egg noodles, mashed potato, or plain boiled potatoes.

A wide, heavy casserole or Dutch oven gives the best result: enough surface area to brown the beef in batches, and heavy enough to maintain a steady simmer over the long cook. Thin pots will concentrate heat at the base and scorch the paprika.

Heat {{vegetable-oil}} of vegetable oil in a wide, heavy casserole over a medium-low heat. Add {{onion}} of sliced onion and a pinch of salt. Cook, stirring every few minutes, for 20 to 25 minutes until the onion is very soft, golden, and beginning to caramelise. Do not rush this step — the onion must be fully sweet and soft before the paprika goes in.

Add {{garlic}} of garlic and {{caraway-seeds}} of caraway seeds to the softened onion; stir for 1 minute. Remove the pot from the heat briefly, then stir in {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika and {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée. Return to a low heat and stir for 30 seconds until the paste darkens slightly. Add {{pepper-green}} of sliced green pepper.

Season {{beef-chuck}} of beef chunks well with salt and pepper. Increase the heat to medium-high. Add the beef to the paprika-onion base in batches, stirring to coat each piece in the spiced paste. Once all the beef is in and coated, pour in {{stock-beef}} of beef stock. The stock should almost cover the meat — add a splash of water if needed.

Bring to a gentle simmer, cover with the lid slightly ajar, and cook on the lowest heat for 70 to 80 minutes, stirring every 20 minutes, until the beef is completely tender and the sauce has reduced and thickened. The sauce should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon. Taste for seasoning and adjust with salt. Serve with a spoonful of soured cream if using.

items.cause: The paprika was added before the onion was fully softened, or the heat was too high when the paprika went in.
items.fix: Paprika scorches easily and turns bitter when exposed to high heat without fat or moisture to protect it. Always remove from the heat briefly before adding paprika, and ensure the onion is fully soft and sweet first.

items.symptom: Beef is still tough after 80 minutes
items.cause: The heat was too high and the collagen has not had time to break down, or the beef was cut too large.
items.fix: Continue cooking at a very gentle simmer, checking every 15 minutes. Beef chuck needs a sustained low temperature to become tender — high heat tightens rather than softens it. Add a splash of water if the sauce is drying out.

items.cause: The lid was kept fully on throughout, preventing reduction.
items.fix: Remove the lid and increase the heat slightly for the final 15 minutes. Stir frequently to prevent the base from catching.

items.cause: Old or low-quality paprika was used, or an insufficient amount.
items.fix: Buy Hungarian sweet paprika from a delicatessen if possible — supermarket paprika varies in quality and can be stale. Goulash needs 3 tablespoons for 4 servings; halving it produces a stew that tastes seasoned rather than spiced.

content.text: Goulash originates in the Hungarian Great Plain as a herdsman's dish, originally cooked in a cauldron over open fire and made with dried beef. Sweet paprika did not arrive in Hungary until the sixteenth century via Ottoman traders, but it became so integral to the dish that it is now impossible to imagine one without the other. The stew spread across the Habsburg Empire and into Central and Eastern Europe in multiple variations. In Britain it arrived through postwar migration and continental food writing, and it remains one of the most recognisable of all Central European dishes.
